California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
Ann R. Slaby′  Ph.D.
343 Panoramic Way
Berkeley′  CA  94704
Dear Ms
s Iaby :
MarCh 4′  ■992
Re:
Your Request fOr lnfOrmal Ass■
stance
Our File No. I-92-085
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your respons ibi.I it ies as a nember of Berkeley's zoning
Adjustment Board pursuant to the conf l ict-of - interest provisions
of the Po]itical ieform Act (the rrasgrrl.l Since your request
refers to conduct that has already occurred, we cannot provj.de you
with advice concerning that conduct. Regulation 18329(b) (8) (A) ,
provides that formal !,rritten advice witl be declined r"/here the
rrrequestor is seeking advice relating to past conduct.r! Thus,
notning in this letter should be construed to be an opinion
concerning past conduct. However, v/e can provide the following
informal guidelines i.rith respect to future situations that may
confront you.'
OUESTION
As a menber of Berkeley's Zoning Adjustnent Board, does the
Act prohibit you or your spouse fron interacting $rith.your agency
concLrning the rezoning of real property that is within 300 feet
of real property in which you have an interest?
CONCLUSION
As a public of f icj.a1, you may not make, participate in making
or use your official 
position to influence a governmental decision
1 co,r..n..nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. AIt statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherlui,se indicated. 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations. section
18600, et seo. AIl references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or f orrnal written advice. (section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3) . )
428 J StrcCt,Suite 800 ● P.0.Box 807 ● Sacramcnto CA 95804‐ 0807 ● (916)322‐ 5660


$rhich wiLt have a foreseeable and materiaL financial effect on
your real property. only the official 
can have a conflict of
interest, and the disquat i f ication obligation applies only to the
official's 
invoLvernent in a governmental decision, either directly
or through an agent.
However, an otherwise disqualified official 
nay apPear in the
same nanner as any other mernber of the general public before his
or her own agency to represent his or her own personal interests.
FACTS
You are a member of Berkeley's zoning Adjustment Board. The
board lriII be considering the rezoning of property within 300 feet
of property that you own with your spouse. r 
You have asked to
wnal extenl you and your spouse are restricted from contacting
your agency concerning the proposal.
ANALY S I S
conflicts of fnterest, General Iv
The Act, $ras adopted by the people of the State of California
by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the conf l ict-of-interest
piovisions of the Act was to ensure that public officials, 
whether
etected or appointed, $rouId perforn their duties in an inpartial
nanner, free irom bias caused by their own financiaL interests or
the financial interests of persons uho have supported then.
(section 81001(b) . )
In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits public officials at any level of state or local
|overnment- fron making, participlting 
in rnaking or attempting to
rise their official 
position to influence a governmental decision
if the official 
knows or has reason to knov, the official 
has a
financial interest. I
Section 87103 (b) provides that a public official 
has a
financial interest in i decision if it is reasonabl.y foreseeable
that the decision will have a rnaterial financial effect,
distinguishable frorn the effect on the public aeneral)'y, on any
real pioperty in which the public official 
has a direct or
indirect interest worth S1,000 or nore.
You stated that you have an interest in real property which
is within 3oo feet of property that is the subject of a decision

Page 2
3 Yo, stated in our telephone conversation of February 27, 1992
that the decision was put over to a March neetinq date'
4 Th. conf l ict-of- interest obligations of the Act pertain to the
officiat 
onJ.y, although the econonic interests of a spouse may
also be disqualifYing interests.


Our File No. I-92-085
Page 3
li[Ii:I::g:1:首iとril:i:::[::[I::。1:尊h!:irl]::1:::::::::];:n:l:
materiallv affect your property.
The Commission has adopted a variety of regulations which
provide objeCtiVe standards for determining whether the financial
effect of a decision on an official′
s real property will be
material。  (See oenerallv′  Regulation 
■8702.■, Regulation
■8702.3.)
Regulation ■8702.3(a)(■)provides:
(a) The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an offic■
al has a direct′
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the
fol10Wing applies:
(1)  The real property in which the
offic■al has an ■nterest′  or any part of that
real property′  is located w■thin a 300 f00t
radius of the boundaries (or the proposed
:::;::[1::)th[ :::i:I:::riXlと::CthlSd[::sion
will have no financial effect upon the
official′ s real property interest.
Thus′  if a decision will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on your property interests, you are prohibited
from participating in the decision.
Partic■DatiOn
What constitutes ':participationn in a governmental decision
has been broadly interpreted in furtherance of the goals of the
Act.  Participation includes voting′  making an appointment′
committing an agency tO a course of action′
 entering into a
contractual agreement on behalf of the agency′ determining not to
act′  negOtiating′  advising or making recommendations to the
dec■s■on― maker.
In addition′  if you have a conflict of interest′  you are
prohibited from attempting to influence the governmental decision.
Regulation 18700.■(a)proVides that with regard to a governmental
decision which is within or before the official′ s agency or an
agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of his or
5 An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial Iikelihood that it will occur. certaj.nty is not
required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable. (fn re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops.
re8. )


Our File No. I-92-085
Page 4
her agency, the official 
is attempting to use his or her officiaL
position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears
before, or otherwise attempts to inffuence, any member, officer,
enployee or consultant of the agency. The same rule $/ould apply
where an official 
rnight act throuqh an aqent to influence the
official's 
governmental body.
Hor./ever, Regulation 18700.1 provides several exceptions. For
example, where an otherwise disqualified official 
appears in the
same nanner as any other nenber of the general public before his
or her o$/n agency in the course of its prescribed governmental
function to represent his or her own personal interest in real
propertv wholIy owned by the official or nembers of the official's
immediate family, the conduct is not considered Imaking.
participating in rnaking or influencingtr a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700. 1(b) (1). ) e
Pl.ease note, this exception Iimits your involvelnent to your
own personal interests, and thus, you should take care to clarify
that you are not acting in any official capacity, nor appearing
before your orrrn agency to represent any other person's interests.
(Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-87-151.) l'toreover, you must follow
the procedures that any other member of the public nust follow.
(Lev-inqer Advice Letter, No. I-88-328.) Since your spouse is not
i' puUfic official, 
your sPouse is not subject to these regulations
whln acting independently. However, if your spouse is acting as
vour aqent, his contacts with the agency are subject to the saEe
iestriitions 
which woutd apply to you if you acted directly.
If you have any further questions regardinq this natter,
please feel free to contact me at (915) 322-59oL. '
sincerely,
Scott

public official comrnunicates with the general public or the press,
the official is not attempting to use his or her official position
to influence a governnental decision.
7 copies of Connission regulations and opinions are available in
rnany law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these nateriaLs and
Co:nmission advice letters may be obtained fron the Cornmission at a
cost of 10C per page.
